responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates a fundamental tension: capturing rare, nocturnal physiological events requires months-long continuous observation in a naturalistic domestic environment, yet the clinical rigor needed for publication and funding demands standardized measurement and strict safety oversight. The selected levers prioritize this conflict — balancing participant retention against data yield, minimizing false alarms while detecting genuine events, scaling staffing safely without clinical overhead that suppresses behavior, and sequencing technology deployment to avoid premature complexity. These five levers directly determine whether the unit sustains operationally viable throughput (events captured per admission, unproductive stays, technician burden) and whether resulting phenotyping and algorithm work will be credible and reproducible.

levers.name: Event-Capture Thresholds and Admission Criteria Tightness
levers.consequences: Stricter pre-admission screening (requiring prior diagnostic polysomnography or specialist video documentation of an event, not just collateral history) raises the likelihood each admitted participant will produce a captured event, reducing unproductive admissions and maximizing data yield. However, tighter criteria shrink the eligible population, lengthen recruitment timelines, and may bias the cohort toward severe or treatment-refractory cases, limiting generalizability of phenotyping findings to milder parasomnia presentations seen in primary care.
options: Require documented polysomnographic or specialist-witnessed event prior to admission, accepting a recruitment window of 6–8 months for 50 participants and potentially skewing toward moderate-to-severe phenotypes

Accept clinical interview with collateral history and at minimum one prior screening PSG showing polysomnographic markers of sleep instability, broadening eligibility but accepting a 20–25% unproductive admission ceiling before protocol review

Implement a two-stage funnel: screen initially via interview and prior PSG, then invite only those with baseline actigraphy data from home showing ≥3 fragmentation episodes per week over 2 weeks, maximizing pre-admission predictive signal at the cost of requiring participants to purchase or loan actigraphy equipment before enrollment
levers.review_lever: Stricter criteria reduce false negatives (non-event admissions) but skew recruitment toward the severe end of the phenotypic spectrum, potentially missing the naturalistic progression of milder parasomnias that evolve into clinical presentations. None of the options address the possibility that a participant's event frequency varies seasonally or cyclically; a negative pre-admission screen may falsely exclude someone who will have high event density during winter months.

levers.name: Tiered Sensing Activation and Technician Annotation Burden
levers.consequences: Deploying full PSG on all 21 nights per participant generates comprehensive signal but requires 9 independent scorers at full FTE or equivalent, pushing personnel costs toward infeasibility and creating annotation bottlenecks. Conversely, running full PSG only on strategically chosen nights (first, mid-stay, final, and event-triggered escalation) reduces annotation labor and keeps staffing at 9 FTE, but risks missing event morphology subtleties or mischaracterizing rare variants if they occur outside scheduled windows.
options: Run full PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on every admission night, accepting that independent dual scoring will require 12+ person-months of effort per 30-participant cohort and necessitate hiring a fourth full-time scoring technician or outsourcing to a commercial polysomnography scoring service

Schedule full PSG on first, mid-stay, and final nights only, plus technician-triggered escalation nights after flagged events, running continuous low-burden monitoring (dry EEG headband, mattress sensor, infrared, ambient mic) on intervening nights, keeping independent scoring workload to ~4 person-months per 30-participant cohort

Randomize 40% of participants to a full nightly PSG arm and 60% to the selective PSG schedule, enabling direct within-study comparison of whether full nightly PSG improves event characterization or phenotyping accuracy relative to event-triggered escalation, accepting the inherent statistical power reduction for Aim 2 longitudinal analysis
levers.review_lever: The core tension is whether event morphology—especially rare variants or sub-threshold arousals—requires comprehensive nightly coverage or whether event-triggered escalation captures sufficient detail. The critical unaddressed risk is technician reliability in real-time event flagging: if a technician misses a flagged event during an intervening night, that event will never receive full PSG coverage and will be lost to analysis, biasing the dataset toward more obvious or frequent episodes.

levers.name: Staffing Coverage Model and Participant Safety Margin
levers.consequences: Assigning one night technician per two suites (4 technicians covering 8 active suites) maximizes cost efficiency and keeps personnel budgets predictable, but leaves no margin for absence (illness, vacation) and reduces the technician's capacity to respond rapidly to safety events or annotate concurrently during episodes. A 1:2 ratio also means high cognitive load during event detection and flagging, risking missed episodes or false alarms. A 1:1.6 ratio (5 technicians) or dedicated event-response protocols buffers against coverage gaps and ensures annotation quality but adds ~€200K annually to the budget.
options: Deploy one trained night technician per 2.5 active suites (4 technicians per 8-suite cohort rotating 4 nights per week), retaining the clinical psychologist as an on-call event responder for behavioral de-escalation or safety intervention, capping technician salary and accepting periodic coverage gaps due to illness or vacation

Hire a fifth night technician to enforce a 1:1.6 technician-to-suite ratio during all operational nights, funding the additional €200K annually by deferring suite expansion to year 3 and accepting a slightly smaller year-2 cohort (28–30 participants rather than 30–35), ensuring dual real-time coverage during flagged events

Establish a tiered staffing model: one technician per 2 suites during predicted high-activity periods (first 3 nights, weekends, identified seasonal clusters), and one per 3 suites on low-activity nights, calibrated by data from the pilot 15–20 participant cohort, with outcomes reviewed at month 12 to lock in the optimal ratio for years 2 and 3
levers.review_lever: The tension is between cost control and real-time event response reliability—the budget assumes staffing at the lean end, but overnight parasomnias are unpredictable and concurrent events across multiple suites can overwhelm a single technician. Critically, the options assume that the clinical psychologist's on-call presence is adequate for de-escalation; if a participant becomes acutely agitated or unsafe, the technician's ability to simultaneously document video, annotate EEG, and manage behavior is not addressed, raising liability risk.

levers.name: Low-Burden Sensor Accuracy vs. Full PSG Dependency for Event Validation
levers.consequences: Relying on dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors to flag candidate events reduces operational burden and makes overnight living more naturalistic, but these devices have lower signal fidelity than gold-standard scalp PSG and may generate false positives (movement artifacts misclassified as arousals) or false negatives (subclinical arousals below detection threshold). Escalating to full PSG only after low-burden flagging ensures high-fidelity capture but creates a workflow dependency: if the low-burden sensors fail to detect an event, it will not receive PSG validation and may be lost entirely.
options: Privilege the dry-electrode EEG headband and mattress sensor as primary event detectors, training the event-triage algorithm on their outputs and validating algorithm performance against PSG-confirmed events only on escalation nights, accepting that some genuine but subtle parasomnia events may go undetected if they fall below the sensor sensitivity threshold

Treat low-burden sensors as environmental monitoring aids rather than primary event detectors; use manual review of nightly sensor and video summaries by the night technician to flag suspected events, then escalate to full PSG regardless of sensor concordance, prioritizing human clinical judgment and accepting higher technician annotation time per night

Implement a validation pilot during the first 25 participant admissions: compare dry-electrode vs. full PSG sensitivity and specificity on the same nights using receiver-operating-characteristic curve analysis, then lock in the primary detection strategy for years 2–3 based on empirical sensitivity-specificity trade-off, deferring full decision to month 9 when pilot data mature
levers.review_lever: The tension is between operational simplicity (sensors as primary) and diagnostic fidelity (human review + escalation). A hidden assumption is that the dry-electrode headbands will remain reliably fitted throughout multi-week admissions; poor adherence (slippage, disconnection) is common in real-world home sleep studies and can render low-burden signals uninterpretable, forcing default escalation and inflating costs on some participants while missing events on others with equipment failure.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Scope and Labeling Effort
levers.consequences: Developing a semi-automated classifier to rank candidate events by parasomnia probability and prioritize human review reduces annotation time and false-positive review burden, but requires a labeled training dataset of several hundred manually scored events—a significant upfront investment by independent raters during years 1–2. A broader classifier that incorporates polysomnographic features, video motion, and ambient sound requires deeper feature engineering and more complex model validation, increasing development risk and timeline but potentially improving specificity and reducing human scorer disagreement.
options: Train a lightweight event-ranking model using only features from the dry-electrode headband and mattress sensor (arousal counts, movement magnitude, temporal clustering), validated against dual human scorer consensus on escalation PSG nights, keeping the algorithm simple, fast to develop, and interpretable but limiting its discriminative power

Construct a multi-modal ensemble classifier integrating scalp EEG topography, chin EMG and leg EMG bursts, infrared motion velocity, ambient sound spectrograms, and temporal context (time-of-night, sleep stage), requiring substantial feature engineering and cross-validation but potentially improving precision and reducing false-positive escalation burden on human scorers

Partner with a computational neuroscience group (internal to University of Bonn or external) to develop and validate the classifier, trading reduced in-house algorithm ownership for methodological rigor, faster development, and publication of the model architecture as a co-authored methodological paper, accepting the loss of proprietary algorithmic advancement but gaining external validation credibility
levers.review_lever: The tension is between keeping algorithm development tractable during the research timeline (lightweight, interpretable, ready by month 20) versus building something ambitious enough to substantially reduce human annotation burden (multi-modal ensemble, but at risk of over-engineering and delayed validation). Critically, none of the options address the baseline variability in human scorer agreement itself: if dual scorers disagree on 15–20% of borderline events, training an algorithm to achieve higher concordance than human consensus is mathematically impossible, yet this limitation is rarely acknowledged upfront in method validation planning.

levers.name: Geographic Recruitment and Travel Logistics
levers.consequences: Recruiting participants primarily from the Bonn region and Deutsche Schlafmedizin network minimizes travel burden and increases local follow-up feasibility but may narrow the phenotypic and geographic diversity of the participant cohort, potentially limiting the generalizability of findings to other German or European sleep populations. Expanding recruitment to Switzerland, Austria, or the broader EU to capture broader phenotypic diversity increases travel costs (participant reimbursement, accommodation during screening visits), extends logistical complexity, and may complicate informed consent and data protection compliance across jurisdictions.
options: Restrict recruitment to adults living within 150 km of Bonn and those referred by the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and regional Deutsche Schlafmedizin network contacts, prioritizing local enrollment, minimizing travel reimbursement, and ensuring the PI and core team can conduct in-person screening and enrollment interviews

Establish explicit recruitment targets: 50% from the Bonn region, 30% from broader Germany (coordinating video screening interviews for remote candidates), and 20% from Austria or Switzerland, scaling travel reimbursement budgets and coordination workload but enriching phenotypic diversity and enabling comparative sub-group analysis by geographic recruitment source

Open recruitment conditionally only after the first 25 participants are enrolled: if geographic diversity is low and phenotypic ranges appear narrow by month 12 enrollment review, expand to Austria and Switzerland with dedicated regional recruitment partnerships and video pre-screening, accepting later enrollment start for non-local participants but preserving budget if geographic expansion proves unnecessary
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between recruitment speed and phenotypic diversity. Restricting to the Bonn region and local network enables rapid, cost-efficient enrollment but risks selecting for patients already embedded in the German academic sleep medicine ecosystem—potentially skewing toward more treatment-resistant or complex cases referred through specialist networks rather than primary care presentations. Geographic expansion abroad introduces GDPR compliance and ethical approval complexity (separate review boards in CH and AT) that are underestimated in the current budget.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership Timing and Publication Independence
levers.consequences: Engaging a pharma partner early (year 1) provides upside funding, accelerates algorithm development through industry computational resources, and increases the likelihood of Phase 2 intervention trial funding. However, pharma involvement raises implicit expectations for algorithm performance targets, recruitment timelines, and data access that may conflict with academic publication timelines and methodological conservatism. Conversely, delaying pharma engagement until year 2 (after Aim 1 and early Aim 2 validation) preserves academic independence longer but risks losing industry interest if a competitor publishes similar findings first.
options: Pursue the DFG and University of Bonn internal funding exclusively through year 2, establish publication-ready methods and phenotyping results by month 24, and approach pharma partners from a position of demonstrated methodological rigor and preliminary data maturity, accepting delayed Phase 2 expansion but ensuring full academic autonomy and first-publication priority

Solicit a pharma partnership by month 4–6 (after ethics approval and pilot cohort recruitment), negotiate a formal collaboration agreement with explicit carve-outs for academic publication rights, independent publication timing (no pharma veto, only a 30-day comment window per the plan), and co-authored methodology papers, accelerating computational and enrollment support but introducing governance oversight and implicit timelines

Structure a phased industry engagement: secure a non-binding letter of intent from a pharma partner by month 6 contingent on Aim 1 validation (safe, sustainable operations for 15+ participants), then finalize a full collaboration agreement by month 12 only if preliminary Aim 2 data demonstrate sufficient event capture and phenotypic resolution, preserving maximum autonomy during the highest-uncertainty phase while enabling industry acceleration during the higher-confidence phase
levers.review_lever: The tension is between financial upside and editorial independence. The current plan claims pharma is optional (not a feasibility requirement) and retains full publication rights, but this language rarely survives a real pharma contract negotiation: confidentiality clauses, publication delays for IP review, and implicit expectations for favorable algorithm performance often emerge during formal collaboration discussions. None of the options address what happens if a pharma partner requests algorithm modifications or additional testing that diverges from the academic research protocol—an unanticipated scope creep risk.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia unit navigates a fundamental tension between environmental authenticity and scientific rigor. Capturing infrequent nocturnal events demands extended monitoring, yet prolonged stays risk habituation-driven suppression of spontaneous behavior; conversely, intensive PSG nights ensure clinical-grade data but impose cost and participant burden that may skew enrollment toward milder phenotypes. The core levers address how to balance ecological validity against capture yield, allocate scarce technician annotation labor across routine and escalation nights, sequence stay extensions without inflating unproductive admissions, structure pharmaceutical partnerships without compromising publication autonomy, and design the control cohort to unlock algorithmic benchmarking while minimizing recruitment drag. These choices directly determine whether the unit produces generalizable phenotypes or remains confined to a self-selected, over-instrumented sample.

levers.name: Residential Stay Duration Architecture and Event-Yield Targets
levers.consequences: Longer stays (6–8 weeks versus 2–4 weeks) increase capture probability for infrequent events and longitudinal pattern detection, but extend per-participant cost, occupancy time, and risk of behavioral habituation as participants adapt to the residential environment. Shorter stays reduce cost and facility throughput but require tighter pre-admission prediction of event frequency and risk capping the cohort at milder, more predictable phenotypes.
options: Implement fixed 3-week baseline admissions for all participants, with event-yield tracked against pre-screening self-reported frequency, and extension to weeks 4–6 triggered only for participants with zero captured events after week 3, capped at 10 weeks total per protocol.

Adopt a stratified entry model where participants reporting ≥4 episodes per month enter for 2 weeks, those reporting 2–3 episodes per month enter for 4 weeks, and those on the inclusion threshold enter for 5 weeks, with no extension option to preserve participant selection homogeneity and simplify operational forecasting.

Offer all participants a standard 6-week residential window upfront with explicit flexibility to depart early if desired, accepting higher baseline occupancy costs but enabling genuine longitudinal pattern detection and reducing the perceived research burden perception that might skew volunteer self-selection.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is balancing event capture against cost efficiency and sample representativeness. All three options assume that event-capture probability scales predictably with duration and prior self-report frequency; however, the pre-admission interview and partner collateral history are subject to recall bias and symptom underestimation, particularly among participants with infrequent arousals or minimal awareness of their own episodes. None of the options address how to validate or recalibrate pre-admission estimates after the first night, which could unlock better-targeted extension decisions without waiting until week 3 to detect non-capture.

levers.name: Night-Technician Annotation Labor Allocation and Real-Time Scoring Depth
levers.consequences: Requiring real-time technician annotation of all low-burden sensor streams and mattress data during every shift maximizes capture responsiveness but depletes technician focus and introduces fatigue-driven scoring inconsistency after midnight. Deferring most annotation to post-hoc batches with scheduled technician tagging only on enhanced PSG nights reduces fatigue but risks missing early event signals that could trigger escalation nights opportunistically.
options: Assign technician real-time annotation exclusively to enhanced PSG nights (first, mid-stay, final) and escalation nights, with nightly low-burden sensor triage performed asynchronously in 8-hour batches during business hours using a standardized checklist, reserving technician night-shift attention for safety monitoring and participant support rather than active scoring.

Deploy a hybrid annotation protocol where technicians flag suspected events in real time using a simplified sensor rubric (e.g., mattress movement + audio cues + camera motion) only when thresholds are crossed, automatically triggering escalation nights, with formal AASM scoring deferred to post-hoc dual-rater review of flagged epochs and a random 10% audit of non-flagged nights to detect false negatives.

Maintain continuous real-time technician annotation of all sensor streams on all nights using staggered night-shift overlap (6-hour paired shifts with 2-hour handoff window) to distribute cognitive load and sustain annotation consistency, accepting the higher staffing cost as essential for maximizing intra-night event detection and precise escalation timing.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between annotation burden and signal fidelity. All options treat technician fatigue and detection consistency as discrete variables, but none account for the fact that real-time annotation during monotonous low-burden monitoring (hours 2–5 AM with no event activity) may paradoxically impair technician alertness when an actual event occurs, whereas asynchronous batch review performed during alert morning hours might achieve higher sensitivity for subtle sensor patterns. The trade-off between fatigue-driven errors and post-hoc detection delay is not directly addressed.

levers.name: Enhanced-Night PSG Scheduling and Technician Workload Concentration
levers.consequences: Concentrating PSG montage on three fixed nights per stay (entry, mid, exit) minimizes technician electrode preparation time but risks missing spontaneous clusters or triggers that fall outside the scheduled window, potentially underestimating true parasomnia frequency. Flexible or event-triggered PSG deployment captures more naturalistic patterns but requires technicians to rapidly set up complex montages reactively, introducing setup errors and downtime that compound across multiple participants per night.
options: Maintain three fixed PSG nights per participant (night 1, mid-stay day 10–14, night before discharge) regardless of event detection on low-burden sensors, ensuring consistent technician workflow and staffing predictability, with the explicit trade-off that some within-person clusters or triggers occurring outside these windows will remain characterizable only via low-burden sensor morphology and inter-rater qualitative agreement on video.

Schedule PSG montage contingent on technician-flagged events during low-burden monitoring in the preceding 48 hours, such that an escalation night receives full PSG setup within 24 hours of a detected suspected event, plus mandatory PSG on night 1 and discharge night only if no prior escalation occurred, allowing technician effort to concentrate on nights with highest signal-to-noise ratio.

Implement a rotating PSG-duty schedule where one technician is designated nightly for rapid PSG setup on escalation nights, while the other two focus on low-burden sensor annotation and participant support, supported by an on-call trained assistant hired for reactive escalation nights, distributing PSG preparation load and enabling same-night recurrence capture without routine setup overhead.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is balancing predictability of technician workload against capture of naturalistic event clustering. All options assume technician setup speed and accuracy remain stable whether PSG montage is routine (learned, fast) or reactive (novel sequence, error-prone); in practice, reactive montage setups may introduce more electrode repositioning errors and missed channels on nights of highest clinical urgency. None of the options explicitly cost the cognitive and equipment-verification load that comes with switching between routine and reactive protocols on the same shift.

levers.name: Data Sharing Scope and Publication Embargoes for Pharmaceutical Partnerships
levers.consequences: Granting pharma partners early access to de-identified physiological data (ECG, respiratory, limb movement) before publication enables faster secondary analyses and co-authored pharma-sponsored work but risks publication pressure or prioritized pathways that disadvantage investigator-initiated research directions. A strict 30-day pre-publication comment window maximizes publication autonomy but forfeits potential co-authorship opportunities and may discourage pharma co-funding interest.
options: Offer pharma partners a 12-month data embargo on physiological datasets following completion of data collection, with access to preliminary summary statistics (event frequency, demographics, sensor performance metrics) during years 1–2, contingent on a written co-publishing agreement explicitly prohibiting pharma from proposing competing manuscripts on the same data for 6 months after the primary investigator's first submission.

Implement a 30-day pre-publication comment window only, with no advanced data access, contingent on pharma funding covering a fixed €250K of infrastructure costs (network storage, server maintenance, independent scoring) in year 2, framed as a service agreement rather than data partnership, preserving complete publication autonomy and investigator control of analysis direction.

Negotiate staggered data-release tiers: pharma receives de-identified physiological waveforms and event timestamps after the primary methods-validation publication (≈month 18), excluding any raw video or face-associated metadata, with explicit restriction to exploratory secondary analyses that do not overlap with the investigator's registered Aim 2 phenotyping hypotheses for 18 months.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between funding leverage and research independence. All options assume pharma's interest is primarily data-driven; however, pharmaceutical partners often view sleep physiology research as a preclinical validation pathway for compound efficacy and may exert subtle (or explicit) pressure to focus on pharmacological mechanisms or patient subtypes aligned with their pipeline, regardless of formal contractual language. None of the options address how to handle disputes over whether a pharma-proposed analysis competes with investigator-directed work, or what governance body adjudicates such conflicts.

levers.name: Matched Control Cohort Sampling and Recruitment Burden
levers.consequences: Recruiting age- and sex-matched healthy controls on identical 2–6 week residential protocols enables direct sensor benchmarking and reduces algorithmic false-positive rates, but requires active recruitment of asymptomatic volunteers and doubles facility occupancy planning complexity. Alternatively, using historical healthy-sleeper datasets or brief ambulatory controls reduces recruitment burden but sacrifices environmental consistency and limits the training set available for event-triage algorithm development.
options: Recruit 15–20 matched controls using the same channels as parasomnia participants (sleep clinic patient networks, neurology referrals, DGSM member requests) with identical 3-week residential admissions, screened via clinical interview and home sleep apnea rule-out to ensure true health, stored in parallel protocols under the same data governance, enabling direct algorithm evaluation on matched short-term nocturnal sensor baselines.

Partner with an existing healthy-sleeper cohort study at University Hospital Bonn or a neighboring sleep center to access de-identified sensor files (or recruit 8–10 controls for single-night PSG under a separate minimal-risk protocol) recorded under conditions approximating the study's low-burden sensor setup, reducing local recruitment burden and cost while accepting imperfect temporal and environmental matching.

Leverage existing open-access polysomnography datasets (e.g., PhysioNet, MASS) filtered for age-matched healthy participants and supplemented by recruiting 5–8 local controls for a single instrumented residential night to benchmark low-burden sensor false-positive rates under the study's exact acoustic and environmental conditions, accepting lower granularity of long-term nocturnal behavior patterns.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is control-group representativeness against recruitment and occupancy cost. All options assume that matched controls will exhibit stable baseline arousal and movement patterns across a 2–6 week stay; however, even healthy participants may experience sleep perturbation or shift in their behavior during extended residential monitoring (reverse of parasomnia suppression), and asymptomatic controls self-selected for research participation may under-represent true population variability in nocturnal microarousals and movement. None of the options account for the possibility that a control cohort recruited through clinical channels (sleep clinic patient networks) may harbor undetected mild sleep apnea or other subclinical pathology that contaminates the algorithmic 'normal' baseline.

levers.name: Escalation-Night Decision Logic and False-Alarm Cost Tolerance
levers.consequences: Strict escalation criteria (e.g., mattress + audio + video concurrence) minimize unnecessary PSG setups and technician burden but risk false negatives when subtle sensor patterns precede overt movement. Loose criteria (any single sensor anomaly flagged) increase capture sensitivity but generate false-positive escalations that waste technician labor and may desensitize staff to genuine events over time.
options: Define escalation triggers as conjunction of two or more independent sensor streams (e.g., mattress movement + audio vocalization, or video motion + elevated heart rate on contact sensors) within a 30-second window, with technician discretion to override if behavioral context suggests high likelihood, ensuring specificity at the risk of missing single-modality subtle events.

Implement probabilistic escalation scoring where each sensor modality contributes weighted evidence (mattress movement 40%, audio anomaly 35%, video motion 25%), with escalation triggered when combined score exceeds a threshold (e.g., 0.70) tuned empirically during the first 5 participants to balance false-positive rate below 20% per night while maintaining sensitivity above 70% for technician-identified events.

Use a per-participant adaptive threshold where escalation sensitivity is calibrated after the first week based on that individual's baseline sensor patterns (median mattress variance, ambient audio levels, camera motion frequency during sleep), allowing thresholds to drift across participants and across seasons to reduce false alarms without sacrificing detection of genuine within-person variations.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between escalation specificity and within-person sensitivity. All options assume that sensor patterns are stable enough to define meaningful thresholds; however, environmental noise (building HVAC cycling, rain noise, traffic), participant sleep position changes, and natural sleep-stage progression all generate baseline sensor variation that overlaps with early event signatures. None of the options address how to retrain escalation logic when the participant's baseline behavior drifts over a 6–8 week stay, or how to validate that empirically-tuned thresholds from the first 5 participants generalize to subsequent cohorts with different sleep topography and motor patterns.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Assessment and Dual-Scoring Scope
levers.consequences: Requiring independent dual scoring of all flagged and escalation nights by two AASM-trained raters maximizes inter-rater reliability reporting but concentrates expensive scoring labor on nights likely to contain events, potentially inflating apparent reliability. Scoring a random stratified sample of all nights (flagged, non-flagged, escalation) provides more generalizable reliability estimates but requires scoring many event-free hours and reduces precision on the subset of clinically relevant epochs.
options: Score all flagged and escalation nights in full dual-rater format (AASM-trained independent raters, blind to technician flags), report inter-rater agreement specifically on this enriched subset, and score a random 10% of routine low-burden nights to detect false negatives and estimate false-alarm burden, accepting higher cost but enabling precise reliability estimates for the decision-relevant epochs.

Implement a stratified dual-scoring protocol: 100% of escalation nights scored by both raters, 20% of flagged non-escalation nights scored by both raters (randomly sampled), and 5% of routine nights scored by both, with single-rater scoring (secondary rater) of the remaining complement, and inter-rater agreement reported separately for each stratum to characterize scoring precision across event likelihood tiers.

Deploy a cascade scoring architecture where all nights receive single-pass AASM scoring by rater A, with rater B independently scoring only nights where rater A identified at least one parasomnia event, or where technician flags suggest event probability above a threshold, plus a random 5% audit of rater A's event-negative nights to detect discordance, minimizing dual-scoring cost while preserving specificity on the clinically-relevant subset.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is cost-efficient reliability reporting against detection of systematic rater disagreement on subtle or rare morphologies. All options assume inter-rater agreement on clearly-defined AASM parasomnia criteria; however, confusional arousals and sleep terrors occupy a morphological continuum with other arousals, and two experienced raters may exhibit systematic disagreement on borderline cases that affects algorithmic training and validation more than simple kappa statistics reveal. None of the options explicitly budget time for rater discussion and consensus-building on difficult cases, or account for the fact that dual-scoring only flagged nights may artificially inflate apparent agreement if false negatives (genuine events missed by both raters) go undetected.

responses.strategic_rationale: This parasomnia research unit navigates three core tensions: (1) ecological validity versus logistical burden—naturalistic home-like conditions suppress behavior while expanding safety, staffing, and renovation costs; (2) event rarity versus operational sustainability—participants must stay long enough to capture infrequent episodes, but extended residential stays strain budget and staff retention; (3) methodological rigor versus annotation labor—dual human scoring ensures ground truth for algorithm benchmarking, but technician night-shift workload and independent rater availability directly constrain enrollment pace and publication timeline. The levers below target these trade-offs by forcing choices on stay architecture, data governance, risk acceptance, external collaboration models, and resource sequencing—decisions that ripple through recruitment, staffing, publication strategy, and facility solvency.

levers.name: Cohort Heterogeneity and Phenotype Specificity Trade-Off
levers.consequences: Restricting admission to a single parasomnia phenotype (sleepwalking only, or confusional arousals only) dramatically simplifies event-triage algorithm training and inter-rater scoring agreements, since episodes are morphologically consistent within the cohort. However, narrowing phenotype shrinks the eligible recruitment pool by an estimated 60–70% based on clinic prevalence data, extending enrollment timelines and delaying publication of Aim 2 characterization work. Conversely, enrolling all three NREM parasomnias together accelerates recruitment but introduces morphologic variability that inflates dual-scorer disagreement and weakens algorithm generalization boundaries.
options: Restrict enrollment to sleepwalking (somnambulism) alone for the first two years, build a single-phenotype event-triage model with robust inter-rater agreement metrics, then in year three design a separate secondary protocol for confusional arousals if funding permits

Enroll NREM parasomnia participants across all three presentations simultaneously and explicitly stratify all downstream analyses by phenotype, accepting higher inter-rater disagreement variance in exchange for a genuinely representative NREM parasomnia dataset suitable for future comparative mechanistic studies

Enroll primary phenotype cases (sleepwalking and confusional arousal) but exclude sleep terrors pending preliminary method validation, deferring the most morphologically distinct episodes until the core annotation pipeline is operationally proven and inter-rater protocols are locked
levers.review_lever: Phenotype choice drives both recruitment speed and algorithm robustness, creating a direct trade-off between time-to-first-publication and downstream scientific generalizability. All three options assume that morphologic consistency within phenotype reduces annotation burden, but none addresses the hidden cost of post-hoc phenotype stratification — if you discover during year-two analysis that confusional arousals and sleepwalking episodes are indistinguishable in your sensor data despite textbook clinical definitions, you cannot retroactively enroll purer sleepwalking cases to validate the distinction.

levers.name: Independent Rater Recruitment Model and Scoring Bottleneck
levers.consequences: Hiring two dedicated full-time sleep medicine physicians to score all polysomnography in real time during year one locks in consistent inter-rater pairing and rapid feedback loops for algorithm development, but consumes €150K–€180K in personnel and leaves only 30–40 hours per week of actual scoring capacity given clinical duties. Outsourcing dual-scoring to a specialist sleep lab or university contract raters saves recurring payroll but introduces scheduling delays, reduces algorithm feedback latency, and creates variable inter-rater pairs that may inflate disagreement metrics. Hybrid models (one in-house rater plus rotating external consultants) balance cost and continuity but obscure which rater-pair variance reflects true methodological heterogeneity versus person-specific idiosyncrasy.
options: Hire two part-time board-certified sleep medicine physicians (0.5 FTE each) embedded within the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, leveraging existing institutional affiliation and clinic infrastructure to front-load scoring in months 4–12 while preliminary cohorts are still small, then transition to on-demand external contract scoring once algorithm baseline is established

Commission an external specialized polysomnography scoring consortium contracted to blind-score all nights using a locked protocol document, with dual scorers assigned randomly per-session to minimize scorer-pair dependency and maximize dataset heterogeneity for robust inter-rater reliability estimation

Establish a in-house scoring core with one senior full-time clinical sleep researcher paired with rotating student researchers and postdocs, accepting slower throughput in year one but building institutional expertise and locked scoring protocols that persist across all three years and reduce external coordination overhead
levers.review_lever: Rater sourcing shapes both the speed of algorithm iteration and the statistical validity of inter-rater reliability metrics reported to reviewers and funders. The core tension is whether you want stable rater pairs (which may mask systematic bias) or heterogeneous pairs (which inflate disagreement). None of the options directly address the risk that if a single rater becomes unavailable mid-study (illness, job change, sabbatical), the entire scoring pipeline stalls — contingency training or back-up rater pre-recruitment is absent from all three.

levers.name: Residential Stay Minimum Duration and Event Capture Guarantee Threshold
levers.consequences: Setting a hard minimum stay of 2 weeks guarantees sufficient observation window for most participants (aligned with current planning), but risks extending 15–20% of low-yield participants to 10 weeks, incurring €6,400 in compensation and housing per unproductive admission before protocol ceilings trigger recruitment review. Shortening minimum stay to 5–7 days for quick screening and permitting discharge if zero events are captured by day 7 (rather than the planned 2-week minimum) reduces per-participant cost and accelerates turnover, but sacrifices the within-person stability patterns that Aim 2 explicitly targets and biases the cohort toward high-frequency episodic cases. A probabilistic stay-length model that adjusts expected stay based on self-reported monthly episode frequency at intake offers personalization but demands complex pre-admission risk stratification and may demoralize participants if discharge occurs earlier than anticipated.
options: Enforce a fixed 2-week residential minimum for all NREM parasomnia enrollees regardless of event capture, treating the 10-week extension-ceiling as a hard safety valve and accepting that approximately 1 in 5 participants will reach it, then use unproductive admissions as a quality signal triggering recruitment-criteria tightening rather than stay-length reduction

Implement a 5-day rapid-assessment window with mandatory discharge at day 7 if zero events are captured, directing enrolled participants with persistently zero events to a home-monitoring arm using dry EEG and mattress sensors plus weekly video diaries, converting non-events to usable negative-validation data for algorithm testing

Design intake questionnaires that stratify expected monthly episode frequency into low (2–4 per month), moderate (5–12 per month), and high (>12 per month) tiers, then assign corresponding baseline stays of 3 weeks, 2 weeks, and 1.5 weeks respectively, with written pre-admission disclosure of expected duration and clear escape clauses if episodes cluster early
levers.review_lever: Stay duration directly controls per-participant cost, population representativeness (short stays bias toward frequent episodic cases), and Aim 2 phenotyping depth (longer stays yield within-person variability). The core tension is whether you optimize for enrollment speed and budget efficiency or longitudinal characterization completeness. None of the options transparently address the ethical and operational asymmetry that emerges if participants know they might be discharged early — does early discharge feel like failure or relief, and does that perception distort their symptom self-reporting during intake, biasing your recruitment criteria validation?

levers.name: Algorithm Transparency Versus Deployment Readiness and Clinical Gatekeeping
levers.consequences: Building the semi-automated event-triage tool (Aim 3) explicitly as a decision-support aid that ranks episodes by probability but requires human technician final approval maintains clinical safety and publishable inter-rater agreement metrics, but delegates the triage bottleneck to night technicians whose workload during escalation nights is already high. Designing the tool to output binary pass/fail event classifications with sensitivity and specificity targets acceptable for a clinical screening tool (e.g., ≥90% sensitivity to avoid missing true events) risks overselling algorithmic autonomy and invites regulatory scrutiny or misuse by downstream users. A hybrid transparency model—publishing sensitivity/specificity for research audiences while internally documenting false-positive rates and failure modes—enables academic credibility but risks future liability if clinicians adopt the tool outside the research context without understanding its limitations.
options: Publish the event-triage tool as a ranked-probability decision-support system with explicit inter-rater agreement benchmarks and sensitivity/specificity curves stratified by parasomnia phenotype, requiring human technician sign-off on all flagged events and positioning the tool as a research prototype that reduces human review workload but does not claim autonomous diagnosis

Develop the event-triage model using a threshold-optimization framework that maximizes sensitivity (prioritizing false positives over false negatives to avoid missed events), explicitly acknowledge in publications that clinical deployment would require prospective validation in a separate operational cohort, and publish failure case studies to highlight phenotype-specific brittleness

Design the tool using an interpretable machine-learning approach (e.g., decision trees, rule-based features derived from domain expert consensus) rather than black-box neural networks, trade some accuracy for explainability, and position the output as providing clinically interpretable feature-rankings that technicians can reason about and override, supporting a culture of informed skepticism rather than algorithmic authority
levers.review_lever: Algorithm design for a research setting sits at the intersection of scientific rigor, publication appeal, and potential downstream misuse. The core tension is that sensitivity-optimized models for research (which maximize event capture) differ fundamentally from specificity-optimized models for clinical screening (which minimize false alarms in a patient population). None of the options directly address the temporal mismatch: if you train the algorithm on year-one and year-two data, then test it on year-three participants, and its year-three performance is noticeably worse, you have no time to retrain or diagnose whether the degradation reflects true domain shift (e.g., cohort differences) or overfitting to year-one idiosyncrasies.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Governance and Patent Ownership Boundaries
levers.consequences: Formalizing a pharma partnership early (year one) with explicit data-sharing, publication-delay, and patent-claim agreements accelerates funding, provides commercial upside motivation for algorithm development, and potentially enables phase-two scale-up resources. However, negotiating pharma terms consumes 3–6 months of legal and administrative overhead, constrains publication independence (the planned 30-day comment window may expand to 60–90 days under pressure), and introduces conflict-of-interest management complexity if a pharma partner's investigational drug becomes relevant to parasomnia pathophysiology. Deferring partnership until year three (after methods validation publications are complete) preserves academic freedom and publication timing but sacrifices funding upside and risks losing commercial interest if the algorithm performs modestly or if the pharma partner's therapeutic timeline diverges from your research cadence.
options: Secure a pharmaceutical partnership in Q2 2026 with explicit clauses limiting pharma veto rights to a 30-day pre-publication review window, prohibiting patent claims on core methods or algorithms (only on disease-specific or drug-specific applications), and requiring joint publication of negative results if the partner's investigational agent is tested, trading some funding risk for full methodological independence

Reject pharma partnership at inception and instead secure DFG funding as the sole financial pillar, publishing freely on your own timeline and retaining all intellectual property, accepting tighter year-two and year-three budgets (€800K operating cap in year two rather than planned €1.2M) and deferring advanced algorithm work or facility expansion until external grant cycles allow reinvestment

Establish a research collaboration with a pharma partner starting year two (post-methods validation), with the explicit agreement that the partner may conduct a small drug-intervention sub-study (n=15–20 participants) using your residential infrastructure, sharing infrastructure costs in exchange for delayed publication rights (90 days) only on drug-related data, leaving core parasomnia characterization publications unencumbered
levers.review_lever: Pharma partnerships trade funding acceleration and commercial credibility against publication independence and intellectual property control. The core tension is that if you defer partnership until year three, you may signal to funders that early methods validation was insufficient to attract industry interest, weakening your phase-two grant narrative. None of the options address the hidden operational risk: if a pharma partner funds a sub-study and that sub-study generates an unexpected adverse event, your facility becomes entangled in pharmacovigilance reporting and potential regulatory scrutiny, diverting PI attention from primary aims and complicating your relationship with local ethics boards.

levers.name: Night Technician Cross-Training Scope and Single-Point-of-Failure Risk
levers.consequences: Intensive cross-training of all three night technicians in full polysomnography montage interpretation, event annotation, and escalation-night PSG deployment maximizes redundancy and enables any technician to manage complex nights independently, but adds 2–3 months to ramp-up and requires ongoing continuing education funding (€5K–€8K annually per technician). Specialized assignment (one technician trained in escalation nights, one in routine annotation, one in equipment troubleshooting) accelerates deployment but creates single-point-of-failure risk: if the escalation-night specialist is sick or leaves, you cannot deploy full PSG that night, forcing either participant safety compromises or night cancellations. A hybrid model with core-competency cross-training (all technicians can manage tier-one low-burden monitoring and basic annotation, one designated lead for escalation nights) balances risk and cost but may not fully mitigate bottlenecks during understaffed weeks.
options: Cross-train all three night technicians to full PSG-annotation and escalation-night readiness within the first 6 months, requiring each technician to complete a formal sleep medicine technologist certification program (AASM-aligned or equivalent German standard), allocating €15K in training costs and 1 week of paid study leave per technician, accepting higher upfront investment for maximum operational resilience

Assign specialized roles: one technician as the primary escalation-night PSG lead with advanced training, one as the routine-annotation specialist, and one as the equipment/infrastructure manager, establish a clear backup protocol where the equipment manager can execute escalation nights at 70% proficiency if the primary lead is unavailable, accepting some skill degradation in backup scenarios

Hire a fourth part-time night technician (0.5 FTE) starting in Q1 2027 once the pilot cohort validates that three technicians are undersaturated during low-enrollment periods, using the fourth position as a cross-training pipeline — the newer hire rotates through all three roles under supervision, ensuring that by year-three expansion to 12 suites, four fully trained technicians are available
levers.review_lever: Technician staffing is the operational lynchpin of the entire unit, since they execute event capture, real-time annotation, and safety response every night. The core tension is that cross-training takes calendar time upfront but de-risks operational continuity, while specialization accelerates deployment but creates fragile dependency. None of the options directly account for the human-factors risk: if you force overly intensive cross-training on all technicians simultaneously, retention may suffer because technicians feel over-burdened, and if you discover in month 10 that one technician has secretly scheduled a 3-month sabbatical starting month 14, you have no time to recruit and onboard a replacement before your staffing ratios collapse.

levers.name: Data Deposit Timing and Repository Selection for Accessibility Versus Confidentiality Risk
levers.consequences: Depositing de-identified physiological and sensor data in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) immediately after publication (month 24–30) maximizes research transparency, accelerates downstream meta-analyses, and satisfies funder mandates for open data, but requires meticulous de-identification protocols and creates residual re-identification risk if longitudinal sleep patterns or unique sensor signatures allow inference of identity. Delaying public deposit until year five or later (after a 2–3 year embargo) reduces re-identification pressure and allows your team to extract maximal competitive advantage from the dataset, but defers community benefit and may violate funder expectations, inviting compliance audits. A tiered-access model (immediate public deposit of aggregate statistics and algorithm benchmarks, delayed deposit of raw sensor streams to a controlled-access archive requiring data use agreements) splits the difference but adds administrative burden and may confuse potential secondary-use researchers about what is available when.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological time series and sensor metadata openly in OpenNeuro under BIDS standard immediately after each journal publication milestone (methods paper at month 24, Aim 2 characterization paper at month 30), applying stringent de-identification verification (frequency-domain re-identification testing, temporal pattern analysis) and accepting residual risk in exchange for maximal research transparency and funder compliance

Implement a two-tier deposit strategy: publish aggregate summary statistics, inter-rater agreement curves, and algorithm sensitivity/specificity benchmarks in an open preprint and supplemental repository by month 24, but restrict raw polysomnography and video-linked sensor streams to a university-managed controlled-access archive requiring signed data use agreements until month 36, with full open release scheduled for month 48 (allowing competitive publication window)

Negotiate a waiver from your funder (DFG) and ethics board permitting a 3-year publication embargo on all raw physiological data, depositing only code, algorithm models, and de-identified phenotypic summaries immediately, and releasing raw sensor streams only upon direct request to your PI through a transparent governance process, explicitly documenting re-identification concerns as the operational justification
levers.review_lever: Data deposit strategy reflects fundamental tensions between research transparency, researcher competitive advantage, and privacy protection. The core tension is that 'de-identification' is a process with residual risk, not a binary state—longitudinal polysomnography from an insomniac in Bonn may be unique enough to re-identify even if name and date-of-birth are stripped. None of the options transparently cost the administrative burden of managing multi-tier access: if you adopt tiered deposit, you must staff a data custodian role (0.3–0.5 FTE) who handles data use agreement review, access provisioning, and tracking secondary-use publications—a hidden recurring expense not budgeted in the current €3.8M plan.

levers.name: Event-Capture Thresholds and Admission Criteria Tightness
levers.consequences: Stricter pre-admission screening (requiring prior diagnostic polysomnography or specialist video documentation of an event, not just collateral history) raises the likelihood each admitted participant will produce a captured event, reducing unproductive admissions and maximizing data yield. However, tighter criteria shrink the eligible population, lengthen recruitment timelines, and may bias the cohort toward severe or treatment-refractory cases, limiting generalizability of phenotyping findings to milder parasomnia presentations seen in primary care.
options: Require documented polysomnographic or specialist-witnessed event prior to admission, accepting a recruitment window of 6–8 months for 50 participants and potentially skewing toward moderate-to-severe phenotypes

Accept clinical interview with collateral history and at minimum one prior screening PSG showing polysomnographic markers of sleep instability, broadening eligibility but accepting a 20–25% unproductive admission ceiling before protocol review

Implement a two-stage funnel: screen initially via interview and prior PSG, then invite only those with baseline actigraphy data from home showing ≥3 fragmentation episodes per week over 2 weeks, maximizing pre-admission predictive signal at the cost of requiring participants to purchase or loan actigraphy equipment before enrollment
levers.review: Stricter criteria reduce false negatives (non-event admissions) but skew recruitment toward the severe end of the phenotypic spectrum, potentially missing the naturalistic progression of milder parasomnias that evolve into clinical presentations. None of the options address the possibility that a participant's event frequency varies seasonally or cyclically; a negative pre-admission screen may falsely exclude someone who will have high event density during winter months.

levers.name: Tiered Sensing Activation and Technician Annotation Burden
levers.consequences: Deploying full PSG on all 21 nights per participant generates comprehensive signal but requires 9 independent scorers at full FTE or equivalent, pushing personnel costs toward infeasibility and creating annotation bottlenecks. Conversely, running full PSG only on strategically chosen nights (first, mid-stay, final, and event-triggered escalation) reduces annotation labor and keeps staffing at 9 FTE, but risks missing event morphology subtleties or mischaracterizing rare variants if they occur outside scheduled windows.
options: Run full PSG montage (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on every admission night, accepting that independent dual scoring will require 12+ person-months of effort per 30-participant cohort and necessitate hiring a fourth full-time scoring technician or outsourcing to a commercial polysomnography scoring service

Schedule full PSG on first, mid-stay, and final nights only, plus technician-triggered escalation nights after flagged events, running continuous low-burden monitoring (dry EEG headband, mattress sensor, infrared, ambient mic) on intervening nights, keeping independent scoring workload to ~4 person-months per 30-participant cohort

Randomize 40% of participants to a full nightly PSG arm and 60% to the selective PSG schedule, enabling direct within-study comparison of whether full nightly PSG improves event characterization or phenotyping accuracy relative to event-triggered escalation, accepting the inherent statistical power reduction for Aim 2 longitudinal analysis
levers.review: The core tension is whether event morphology—especially rare variants or sub-threshold arousals—requires comprehensive nightly coverage or whether event-triggered escalation captures sufficient detail. The critical unaddressed risk is technician reliability in real-time event flagging: if a technician misses a flagged event during an intervening night, that event will never receive full PSG coverage and will be lost to analysis, biasing the dataset toward more obvious or frequent episodes.

levers.name: Staffing Coverage Model and Participant Safety Margin
levers.consequences: Assigning one night technician per two suites (4 technicians covering 8 active suites) maximizes cost efficiency and keeps personnel budgets predictable, but leaves no margin for absence (illness, vacation) and reduces the technician's capacity to respond rapidly to safety events or annotate concurrently during episodes. A 1:2 ratio also means high cognitive load during event detection and flagging, risking missed episodes or false alarms. A 1:1.6 ratio (5 technicians) or dedicated event-response protocols buffers against coverage gaps and ensures annotation quality but adds ~€200K annually to the budget.
options: Deploy one trained night technician per 2.5 active suites (4 technicians per 8-suite cohort rotating 4 nights per week), retaining the clinical psychologist as an on-call event responder for behavioral de-escalation or safety intervention, capping technician salary and accepting periodic coverage gaps due to illness or vacation

Hire a fifth night technician to enforce a 1:1.6 technician-to-suite ratio during all operational nights, funding the additional €200K annually by deferring suite expansion to year 3 and accepting a slightly smaller year-2 cohort (28–30 participants rather than 30–35), ensuring dual real-time coverage during flagged events

Establish a tiered staffing model: one technician per 2 suites during predicted high-activity periods (first 3 nights, weekends, identified seasonal clusters), and one per 3 suites on low-activity nights, calibrated by data from the pilot 15–20 participant cohort, with outcomes reviewed at month 12 to lock in the optimal ratio for years 2 and 3
levers.review: The tension is between cost control and real-time event response reliability—the budget assumes staffing at the lean end, but overnight parasomnias are unpredictable and concurrent events across multiple suites can overwhelm a single technician. Critically, the options assume that the clinical psychologist's on-call presence is adequate for de-escalation; if a participant becomes acutely agitated or unsafe, the technician's ability to simultaneously document video, annotate EEG, and manage behavior is not addressed, raising liability risk.

levers.name: Low-Burden Sensor Accuracy vs. Full PSG Dependency for Event Validation
levers.consequences: Relying on dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors to flag candidate events reduces operational burden and makes overnight living more naturalistic, but these devices have lower signal fidelity than gold-standard scalp PSG and may generate false positives (movement artifacts misclassified as arousals) or false negatives (subclinical arousals below detection threshold). Escalating to full PSG only after low-burden flagging ensures high-fidelity capture but creates a workflow dependency: if the low-burden sensors fail to detect an event, it will not receive PSG validation and may be lost entirely.
options: Privilege the dry-electrode EEG headband and mattress sensor as primary event detectors, training the event-triage algorithm on their outputs and validating algorithm performance against PSG-confirmed events only on escalation nights, accepting that some genuine but subtle parasomnia events may go undetected if they fall below the sensor sensitivity threshold

Treat low-burden sensors as environmental monitoring aids rather than primary event detectors; use manual review of nightly sensor and video summaries by the night technician to flag suspected events, then escalate to full PSG regardless of sensor concordance, prioritizing human clinical judgment and accepting higher technician annotation time per night

Implement a validation pilot during the first 25 participant admissions: compare dry-electrode vs. full PSG sensitivity and specificity on the same nights using receiver-operating-characteristic curve analysis, then lock in the primary detection strategy for years 2–3 based on empirical sensitivity-specificity trade-off, deferring full decision to month 9 when pilot data mature
levers.review: The tension is between operational simplicity (sensors as primary) and diagnostic fidelity (human review + escalation). A hidden assumption is that the dry-electrode headbands will remain reliably fitted throughout multi-week admissions; poor adherence (slippage, disconnection) is common in real-world home sleep studies and can render low-burden signals uninterpretable, forcing default escalation and inflating costs on some participants while missing events on others with equipment failure.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Scope and Labeling Effort
levers.consequences: Developing a semi-automated classifier to rank candidate events by parasomnia probability and prioritize human review reduces annotation time and false-positive review burden, but requires a labeled training dataset of several hundred manually scored events—a significant upfront investment by independent raters during years 1–2. A broader classifier that incorporates polysomnographic features, video motion, and ambient sound requires deeper feature engineering and more complex model validation, increasing development risk and timeline but potentially improving specificity and reducing human scorer disagreement.
options: Train a lightweight event-ranking model using only features from the dry-electrode headband and mattress sensor (arousal counts, movement magnitude, temporal clustering), validated against dual human scorer consensus on escalation PSG nights, keeping the algorithm simple, fast to develop, and interpretable but limiting its discriminative power

Construct a multi-modal ensemble classifier integrating scalp EEG topography, chin EMG and leg EMG bursts, infrared motion velocity, ambient sound spectrograms, and temporal context (time-of-night, sleep stage), requiring substantial feature engineering and cross-validation but potentially improving precision and reducing false-positive escalation burden on human scorers

Partner with a computational neuroscience group (internal to University of Bonn or external) to develop and validate the classifier, trading reduced in-house algorithm ownership for methodological rigor, faster development, and publication of the model architecture as a co-authored methodological paper, accepting the loss of proprietary algorithmic advancement but gaining external validation credibility
levers.review: The tension is between keeping algorithm development tractable during the research timeline (lightweight, interpretable, ready by month 20) versus building something ambitious enough to substantially reduce human annotation burden (multi-modal ensemble, but at risk of over-engineering and delayed validation). Critically, none of the options address the baseline variability in human scorer agreement itself: if dual scorers disagree on 15–20% of borderline events, training an algorithm to achieve higher concordance than human consensus is mathematically impossible, yet this limitation is rarely acknowledged upfront in method validation planning.

levers.name: Geographic Recruitment and Travel Logistics
levers.consequences: Recruiting participants primarily from the Bonn region and Deutsche Schlafmedizin network minimizes travel burden and increases local follow-up feasibility but may narrow the phenotypic and geographic diversity of the participant cohort, potentially limiting the generalizability of findings to other German or European sleep populations. Expanding recruitment to Switzerland, Austria, or the broader EU to capture broader phenotypic diversity increases travel costs (participant reimbursement, accommodation during screening visits), extends logistical complexity, and may complicate informed consent and data protection compliance across jurisdictions.
options: Restrict recruitment to adults living within 150 km of Bonn and those referred by the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and regional Deutsche Schlafmedizin network contacts, prioritizing local enrollment, minimizing travel reimbursement, and ensuring the PI and core team can conduct in-person screening and enrollment interviews

Establish explicit recruitment targets: 50% from the Bonn region, 30% from broader Germany (coordinating video screening interviews for remote candidates), and 20% from Austria or Switzerland, scaling travel reimbursement budgets and coordination workload but enriching phenotypic diversity and enabling comparative sub-group analysis by geographic recruitment source

Open recruitment conditionally only after the first 25 participants are enrolled: if geographic diversity is low and phenotypic ranges appear narrow by month 12 enrollment review, expand to Austria and Switzerland with dedicated regional recruitment partnerships and video pre-screening, accepting later enrollment start for non-local participants but preserving budget if geographic expansion proves unnecessary
levers.review: The core tension is between recruitment speed and phenotypic diversity. Restricting to the Bonn region and local network enables rapid, cost-efficient enrollment but risks selecting for patients already embedded in the German academic sleep medicine ecosystem—potentially skewing toward more treatment-resistant or complex cases referred through specialist networks rather than primary care presentations. Geographic expansion abroad introduces GDPR compliance and ethical approval complexity (separate review boards in CH and AT) that are underestimated in the current budget.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership Timing and Publication Independence
levers.consequences: Engaging a pharma partner early (year 1) provides upside funding, accelerates algorithm development through industry computational resources, and increases the likelihood of Phase 2 intervention trial funding. However, pharma involvement raises implicit expectations for algorithm performance targets, recruitment timelines, and data access that may conflict with academic publication timelines and methodological conservatism. Conversely, delaying pharma engagement until year 2 (after Aim 1 and early Aim 2 validation) preserves academic independence longer but risks losing industry interest if a competitor publishes similar findings first.
options: Pursue the DFG and University of Bonn internal funding exclusively through year 2, establish publication-ready methods and phenotyping results by month 24, and approach pharma partners from a position of demonstrated methodological rigor and preliminary data maturity, accepting delayed Phase 2 expansion but ensuring full academic autonomy and first-publication priority

Solicit a pharma partnership by month 4–6 (after ethics approval and pilot cohort recruitment), negotiate a formal collaboration agreement with explicit carve-outs for academic publication rights, independent publication timing (no pharma veto, only a 30-day comment window per the plan), and co-authored methodology papers, accelerating computational and enrollment support but introducing governance oversight and implicit timelines

Structure a phased industry engagement: secure a non-binding letter of intent from a pharma partner by month 6 contingent on Aim 1 validation (safe, sustainable operations for 15+ participants), then finalize a full collaboration agreement by month 12 only if preliminary Aim 2 data demonstrate sufficient event capture and phenotypic resolution, preserving maximum autonomy during the highest-uncertainty phase while enabling industry acceleration during the higher-confidence phase
levers.review: The tension is between financial upside and editorial independence. The current plan claims pharma is optional (not a feasibility requirement) and retains full publication rights, but this language rarely survives a real pharma contract negotiation: confidentiality clauses, publication delays for IP review, and implicit expectations for favorable algorithm performance often emerge during formal collaboration discussions. None of the options address what happens if a pharma partner requests algorithm modifications or additional testing that diverges from the academic research protocol—an unanticipated scope creep risk.

levers.name: Residential Stay Duration Architecture and Event-Yield Targets
levers.consequences: Longer stays (6–8 weeks versus 2–4 weeks) increase capture probability for infrequent events and longitudinal pattern detection, but extend per-participant cost, occupancy time, and risk of behavioral habituation as participants adapt to the residential environment. Shorter stays reduce cost and facility throughput but require tighter pre-admission prediction of event frequency and risk capping the cohort at milder, more predictable phenotypes.
options: Implement fixed 3-week baseline admissions for all participants, with event-yield tracked against pre-screening self-reported frequency, and extension to weeks 4–6 triggered only for participants with zero captured events after week 3, capped at 10 weeks total per protocol.

Adopt a stratified entry model where participants reporting ≥4 episodes per month enter for 2 weeks, those reporting 2–3 episodes per month enter for 4 weeks, and those on the inclusion threshold enter for 5 weeks, with no extension option to preserve participant selection homogeneity and simplify operational forecasting.

Offer all participants a standard 6-week residential window upfront with explicit flexibility to depart early if desired, accepting higher baseline occupancy costs but enabling genuine longitudinal pattern detection and reducing the perceived research burden perception that might skew volunteer self-selection.
levers.review: The core tension is balancing event capture against cost efficiency and sample representativeness. All three options assume that event-capture probability scales predictably with duration and prior self-report frequency; however, the pre-admission interview and partner collateral history are subject to recall bias and symptom underestimation, particularly among participants with infrequent arousals or minimal awareness of their own episodes. None of the options address how to validate or recalibrate pre-admission estimates after the first night, which could unlock better-targeted extension decisions without waiting until week 3 to detect non-capture.

levers.name: Night-Technician Annotation Labor Allocation and Real-Time Scoring Depth
levers.consequences: Requiring real-time technician annotation of all low-burden sensor streams and mattress data during every shift maximizes capture responsiveness but depletes technician focus and introduces fatigue-driven scoring inconsistency after midnight. Deferring most annotation to post-hoc batches with scheduled technician tagging only on enhanced PSG nights reduces fatigue but risks missing early event signals that could trigger escalation nights opportunistically.
options: Assign technician real-time annotation exclusively to enhanced PSG nights (first, mid-stay, final) and escalation nights, with nightly low-burden sensor triage performed asynchronously in 8-hour batches during business hours using a standardized checklist, reserving technician night-shift attention for safety monitoring and participant support rather than active scoring.

Deploy a hybrid annotation protocol where technicians flag suspected events in real time using a simplified sensor rubric (e.g., mattress movement + audio cues + camera motion) only when thresholds are crossed, automatically triggering escalation nights, with formal AASM scoring deferred to post-hoc dual-rater review of flagged epochs and a random 10% audit of non-flagged nights to detect false negatives.

Maintain continuous real-time technician annotation of all sensor streams on all nights using staggered night-shift overlap (6-hour paired shifts with 2-hour handoff window) to distribute cognitive load and sustain annotation consistency, accepting the higher staffing cost as essential for maximizing intra-night event detection and precise escalation timing.
levers.review: The core tension is between annotation burden and signal fidelity. All options treat technician fatigue and detection consistency as discrete variables, but none account for the fact that real-time annotation during monotonous low-burden monitoring (hours 2–5 AM with no event activity) may paradoxically impair technician alertness when an actual event occurs, whereas asynchronous batch review performed during alert morning hours might achieve higher sensitivity for subtle sensor patterns. The trade-off between fatigue-driven errors and post-hoc detection delay is not directly addressed.

levers.name: Enhanced-Night PSG Scheduling and Technician Workload Concentration
levers.consequences: Concentrating PSG montage on three fixed nights per stay (entry, mid, exit) minimizes technician electrode preparation time but risks missing spontaneous clusters or triggers that fall outside the scheduled window, potentially underestimating true parasomnia frequency. Flexible or event-triggered PSG deployment captures more naturalistic patterns but requires technicians to rapidly set up complex montages reactively, introducing setup errors and downtime that compound across multiple participants per night.
options: Maintain three fixed PSG nights per participant (night 1, mid-stay day 10–14, night before discharge) regardless of event detection on low-burden sensors, ensuring consistent technician workflow and staffing predictability, with the explicit trade-off that some within-person clusters or triggers occurring outside these windows will remain characterizable only via low-burden sensor morphology and inter-rater qualitative agreement on video.

Schedule PSG montage contingent on technician-flagged events during low-burden monitoring in the preceding 48 hours, such that an escalation night receives full PSG setup within 24 hours of a detected suspected event, plus mandatory PSG on night 1 and discharge night only if no prior escalation occurred, allowing technician effort to concentrate on nights with highest signal-to-noise ratio.

Implement a rotating PSG-duty schedule where one technician is designated nightly for rapid PSG setup on escalation nights, while the other two focus on low-burden sensor annotation and participant support, supported by an on-call trained assistant hired for reactive escalation nights, distributing PSG preparation load and enabling same-night recurrence capture without routine setup overhead.
levers.review: The core tension is balancing predictability of technician workload against capture of naturalistic event clustering. All options assume technician setup speed and accuracy remain stable whether PSG montage is routine (learned, fast) or reactive (novel sequence, error-prone); in practice, reactive montage setups may introduce more electrode repositioning errors and missed channels on nights of highest clinical urgency. None of the options explicitly cost the cognitive and equipment-verification load that comes with switching between routine and reactive protocols on the same shift.

levers.name: Data Sharing Scope and Publication Embargoes for Pharmaceutical Partnerships
